The SGS Group 
 
 
 
SGS Group of Companies is the world’s largest independent, international inspection, testing and 
verification organization 
 
 
 
The Group comprises more than 300 affiliated companies operating in over 140 countries with over 
100,000 employees in more than 1600 offices and laboratories. Founded in 1878, today the SGS Group is 
unique in the international technical services sector, not only through its geographical coverage, but also 
through the comprehensive range of services offered. 
 
 
 
The SGS Group is a fully independent inspection and testing organization with no manufacturing, trading 
or financial interests which could compromise its independence. This, together with a reputation for 
quality, integrity and impartiality, is the basis of the Group’s corporate development.

4. DISCLAIMER 
 
The test results presented in this report relate only to the object tested, and based on submitted technical 
documentation, firmware and actual tests, the results are valid only if the same environment is replicated. 
 
This evaluation has been carried out in a time-boxed Blackbox approach. Thus, as agreed with the client, all 
results in this evaluation report are under the caveat of the given time constraint. SGS cannot warrant that, 
even in case there are no findings, the reviewed product platform is flawless from cybersecurity point of view 
due to the limited depth of analysis performed. 
 
Because risks are changing with the technical development, equipment might not be secure as time goes by. 
It is supplier/manufacturer’s responsibility to continually monitor for, identify and rectify security vulnerabilities 
within products and services they sell, produce, have produced and services they operate as part of the 
product security lifecycle. 
 
This test report is issued under SGS general terms of delivery (available on request and accessible at 
www.sgs.com). Attention is drawn to the limitations of liability, indemnification and jurisdictional issues 
defined therein. Unless otherwise stated: (a) the results shown in this document refer only to the sample(s) 
tested and (b) such sample(s) are retained for three months. This document cannot be reproduced except in 
full, without prior approval of SGS. 
 
Any unauthorized alteration, forgery or falsification of the content or appearance of this report is unlawful and 
offenders may be prosecuted to the fullest extent of the law. 
 
5. INTRODUCTION 
5.1 About Target of Evaluation (TOE) 
 
The ULTRAFORMER MPT produces controlled tissue coagulation below the skin surface (epidermis) within 
the first few millimeters of tissue (dermis) using highly focused, low-energy ultrasound deposition. The 
ULTRAFORMER MPT directs micro-focused acoustic waves to the treatment area at desired depths without 
affecting or requiring a secondary action to protect the skin surface. 
    
Figure 1 TOE Welcome Screen 
In this report, the results of the fuzz and penetration tests are presented.

Report: Penetration Report for ULTRAFORMER MPT
Report No.: F690501-RF-CBS000062 
Version: 2.0 
 
 
CONFIDENTIAL 
 
 
Page 7 of 38 
 
5.2 Information 
 
The physical TOE has been set up in the SGS Korea lab in Sep 2025, and the following deliverables have 
been provided by the client for the assessment to the SGS.

5.3. Condition 
 
This Vulnerability Assessment was conducted without any specific conditions or environmental 
constraints. 
 
5.4 TOE Configuration for Testing Environment 
 
The detailed configuration of each TOE in the testing environment is as follows. 
 
Item 
Description 
Network 
192.168.1.0/24 
Role 
TOE  
Operation system 
Raspberry-pi(Linux) 
Installed Software 
1.2 
Installed Firmware 
-  
 
5.5 Evaluation Scope and Limitations 
 
The target of evaluation (TOE) is ULTRAFORMER MPT.  
 
The security testing activities of the product have been performed in the environment provided and the 
results are valid only if the same environment is replicated. The penetration test activities have been 
performed during the assessment. 
 
The TOE has multiple physical interfaces, most of them are designed for connecting different handpieces.

Report: Penetration Report for ULTRAFORMER MPT
Report No.: F690501-RF-CBS000062 
Version: 2.0 
 
 
CONFIDENTIAL 
 
 
Page 8 of 38 
 
The evaluator analyzed them and concluded that most of the physical ports are connected by special 
cable with dedicated protocols that meant for handpieces. Additionally, it is designed for signal transmit, 
and the traffic didn’t have the sensitive data or personal data.  
 
Physical ports such as USB ports are tested for this TOE. Wi-Fi interface is mainly evaluated by host 
vulnerability scanning tool. USB fuzz testing is performed against the USB ports. The logical ports are 
also the focus of the evaluation. If network protocol such as SSH is opened in TOE samples, brute-force 
attacks will be performed against it. 
 
Additionally, the secure functionality (e.g., secure communication) of the application, the data sink, the 
update procedure and the operating system also the focus of the evaluation.  
 
6. TESTING APPROACH 
The scope of this security assessment was to vulnerability scanning, perform fuzz and penetration test to 
identify security threats and vulnerabilities to the TOE. 
 
SGS offers a comprehensive and adaptable penetration testing service, combining industry best practices with 
our proprietary methodologies. Our approach refers with internationally recognized standards, such as 
OWASP (Open Web Application Security Project), NIST 800-115 Technical Guide to Information Security 
Testing and Assessment, PTES (Penetration Testing Execution Standard), etc.  
 
Leveraging our extensive expertise in cybersecurity assessments, SGS customizes each testing engagement 
to address the specific security needs and complexities of each client’s systems, ensuring a targeted, results-
driven approach for enhanced security outcomes. 
 
The main penetration testing process includes: 
 
•Information collection 
•Vulnerability discovery 
•Exploitation 
•Report 
 
6.1 Fuzz Test 
 
This test is used to determine the possible impact of malformed or unexpected input to the TOE. The input 
can be from external physical interfaces (E.g. USB) or logical interfaces (E.g. textbox in website). 
 
The evaluators analyze and investigate the different interfaces. The off-the-shelf fuzz test software (including 
commercial, open source and self-developed script/tools) were used to perform the fuzz testing. 
 
6.2 Penetration Test 
 
This test is used to determine the possible vulnerabilities, attack vectors and impact from an attacker 
perspective.  
 
The evaluators analyze and investigate the identified vulnerabilities, attack vectors and impact, by using CVSS 
calculation and considering TOE operating environment. 
The off-the-shelf cybersecurity testing software (including commercial, open source and self-developed tools) 
were used to identify threats and vulnerability. 
 
Test against secure communication 
The goal of the evaluation is to test possible communication available and the security posture of the 
communication.  
 
Test against authentication 
The goal of the evaluation is to test the authentication mechanism in TOE.  
It is to test if authentication mechanism is strong enough and possible of authentication bypass.

Report: Penetration Report for ULTRAFORMER MPT
Report No.: F690501-RF-CBS000062 
Version: 2.0 
 
 
CONFIDENTIAL 
 
 
Page 9 of 38 
 
Test against authorization 
The goal of the evaluation is to test authorization mechanism in TOE.  
It is to test if authorization mechanism has been set properly, allowing only authorization user to access the 
resources or perform certain actions. 
 
Test against storage 
The goal of this evaluation is to test the storing of sensitive information in TOE.  
It is to test if confidential information has been stored properly, not allowing access to the information  
 
Test against logging 
The goal of this evaluation is to test logging function of TOE if any sensitive information is in logs.  
 
Test against configuration 
The goal of the evaluation is to test configuration in TOE to avoid misconfiguration.  
 
Test against secure update/boot 
The goal of the evaluation is to test update/upgrade function in TOE if it can withstand against modified file 
update or downgrade and the update files provide valuable information.  
 
6.3 Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS) 
 
The findings contained in this report are rated by means of the Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS). 
The CVSS is an open standard for scoring the severity of vulnerabilities. Created by MITRE, it presents a 
uniform method of rating a threat associated with a vulnerability. The latest version of CVSS is 3.1, released 
in 2019. [FIR19] 
 
The overall score is generated by rating sub scores, which can again be divided into different sections. The 
sections and sub scores for CVSS are explained in Table 1. 
 
The CVSS ranges from 0.0 to 10.0, with 10.0 representing the most critical vulnerability. The mapping of score 
to verdict can be seen in Table 2. The Calculator available here has been used to calculate the CVSS. 
 
 
•

8. PENETRATION TEST DETAILED RESULTS 
 
8.1 Observations 
 
The observations mentioned in this section are not considered as vulnerabilities / weaknesses but are of 
informative nature. 
 
• 
None  
 
8.2 Test Passed Results 
 
8.2.1 USB interface fuzzing 
 
USB interface fuzzing 
Test Number:  
PT-GPCB2509000039CS-001 
 
Description 
 
To verify stability and only documented behavior, the USB interface are tested for stability against 
random input. The fuzzing machine was connected via a USB-to-MicroUSB cable to the USB Kit board 
with a custom firmware pre-installed. On the other end, USB Kit board was connected via USB cable 
to the USB port on the TOE. Combination of data from standards documentation has been simulated. 
 
 
Expected Test Result 
 
There is no crashes or undocumented behavior. 
 
 
Obtained Test Result 
 
It is observed that there are no crashes or undocumented behavior on the TOE during testing. The 
fuzz testing logs have been manually analyzed by evaluator, no crashes or errors were found. 
 
Verdict: 
Pass 
 
8.2.1.1 Screenshots Proof 
 
 
Figure 2 USB fuzz test snippet 
As shown in Figure 2, the USB fuzzer first performs a test to confirm that the device class is supported by

Report: Penetration Report for ULTRAFORMER MPT
Report No.: F690501-RF-CBS000062 
Version: 2.0 
 
 
CONFIDENTIAL 
 
 
Page 13 of 38 
 
the TOE. Then the enumeration and fuzz test are performed afterwards. Evaluator observed no crashes 
or undocumented behavior on the TOE. 
 
8.2.2 RS-232 interface fuzzing 
 
RS-232 interface fuzzing 
Test Number:  
PT-GPCB2509000039CS-002 
 
Description 
 
To verify that the RS-232 interface operates stably and only exhibits behavior defined in the 
documentation, a stability test with random input was performed. An RS-232-to-USB board was 
connected to the RS-232 port of the TOE, and the board was connected to the PC via a USB cable. In 
addition, combinations of data based on the relevant standards documentation were simulated and 
transmitted. 
 
 
Expected Test Result 
 
There is no crashes or undocumented behavior. 
 
 
Obtained Test Result 
 
It is observed that there are no crashes or undocumented behavior on the TOE during testing. The 
fuzz testing logs have been manually analyzed by evaluator, no crashes or errors were found. 
 
Verdict: 
Pass 
 
8.2.2.1 Screenshots Proof 
 
 
Figure 3 RS-232 fuzz test snippet 
As shown in Figure 3, the RS-232 fuzzer is a Python-based BooFuzz tool that injects random values into 
the TOE. The tests were then carried out sequentially according to the defined test cases, and the 
evaluator confirmed that no crashes or undocumented behavior occurred on the TOE during testing.

Report: Penetration Report for ULTRAFORMER MPT
Report No.: F690501-RF-CBS000062 
Version: 2.0 
 
 
CONFIDENTIAL 
 
 
Page 14 of 38 
 
8.2.3 Information in logs 
 
Information in logs 
Test Number:  
PT-GPCB2509000039CS-003 
 
Description 
 
To verify that logs in TOE does not provide beneficial information to attacker, evaluator identified all 
TOE logs and view them in the system. 
 
 
Expected Test Result 
 
The information does not provide beneficial information about TOE implementation or sensitive 
information. 
 
 
Obtained Test Result 
 
It is observed that the logs are encrypted in the system and does not provide any information to 
attacker. Evaluator is unable to find decryption methods or keys inside of the system to decrypt the 
logs. 
 
Verdict: 
Pass 
 
8.2.3.1 Screenshots Proof 
 
 
Figure 4 Snippet of encrypted system logs 
As seen in Figure 4, evaluator observed that all the system logs are encrypted and unable to provide any 
information to attacker.

Report: Penetration Report for ULTRAFORMER MPT
Report No.: F690501-RF-CBS000062 
Version: 2.0 
 
 
CONFIDENTIAL 
 
 
Page 15 of 38 
 
8.2.4 Audit Trail Logging 
 
Audit Trail Logging 
Test Number:  
PT-GPCB2509000039CS-004 
 
Description 
 
To ensure there are audit trails in TOE, evaluator have analyzed the logs in the system. 
 
 
Expected Test Result 
 
Security events such as accounts login and changing of TOE configuration are recorded.  
 
 
Obtained Test Result 
 
It is observed there are audit trail logging existed that allows the developer to investigate in case of 
security incident occurred. 
Verdict: 
Pass 
 
8.2.4.1 Screenshots Proof 
 
 
Figure 5 Snippet of TOE decrypted system logs by developer 
The system logs are encrypted at first as shown in section #8.2.3. With the assistance from developer, 
the system logs are decrypted, and evaluator was able to interpret the logs. The first number in the 
decrypted log show the log id which associate with the type of events followed by other information such 
as timestamp and more.

Report: Penetration Report for ULTRAFORMER MPT
Report No.: F690501-RF-CBS000062 
Version: 2.0 
 
 
CONFIDENTIAL 
 
 
Page 16 of 38 
 
8.2.5 QR code modification 
 
QR code modification 
Test Number:  
PT-GPCB2509000039CS-005 
 
Description 
 
The TOE provided a QR code for user to scan to link to developer homepage. 
To verify the QR code cannot be tampered, evaluator searched for images in the systems. 
 
 
Expected Test Result 
 
The QR code cannot be tampered. 
 
 
Obtained Test Result 
 
It is observed that the system does not contains QR code images. With the assistance of developer, 
evaluator have witnessed the QR code is generated real-time in the source code. Hence, QR code 
cannot be tampered. 
Verdict: 
Pass 
 
8.2.5.1 Screenshots Proof 
 
 
Figure 6 QR Code in TOE

Report: Penetration Report for ULTRAFORMER MPT
Report No.: F690501-RF-CBS000062 
Version: 2.0 
 
 
CONFIDENTIAL 
 
 
Page 17 of 38 
 
 
Figure 7 snippet of Search QR image in TOE system 
As seen in Figure 6, there is QR code in TOE. Evaluator attempted to locate QR code images in the 
system as shown in. Evaluator analyzed all results of QR code searches and unable to find QR code 
images. With assistance of developer, evaluator was able to witness that the QR code was generated in 
real-time at source code level. Hence, there is no tampering can be done to the QR code in TOE. 
 
8.2.6 Network sniffing on License Auto-renewal 
 
Network sniffing on License Auto-renewal 
Test Number:  
PT-GPCB2509000039CS-006 
 
Description 
 
TOE required license to operate the handpieces. TOE provided license auto-renewal feature which 
required TOE to connect to internet. Once it is connected to internet and license expiry, TOE will 
send request to developer and the license renewal process begins. To verify secure communication 
for the auto-renewal, evaluator perform network sniffing during the auto-renewal process. 
 
 
Expected Test Result 
 
The traffic doesn’t contain license information and retrievable files. 
 
 
Obtained Test Result 
 
It is observed that the traffic is encrypted and unable to retrieve any information. The network packets 
captured have been manually analyzed by evaluator. 
Verdict: 
Pass 
 
8.2.6.1 Screenshots Proof

Report: Penetration Report for ULTRAFORMER MPT
Report No.: F690501-RF-CBS000062 
Version: 2.0 
 
 
CONFIDENTIAL 
 
 
Page 18 of 38 
 
 
Figure 8 Traffic between TOE and license server 
As shown in Figure 8, the traffic is encrypted with TLS 1.2. The evaluator captured the network traffic from 
the TOE and manually analyzed the collected data, confirming that the traffic is sufficiently encrypted and, 
apart from information on the remote server, does not provide any meaningful information to an attacker. 
 
8.2.7 Application Password Policy 
 
Application Password Policy 
Test Number:  
PT-GPCB2509000039CS-007 
 
Description 
 
To ensure there is adequate requirement for password policy in TOE, evaluator tested a combination 
of passwords to verify the requirement in TOE.  
 
 
Expected Test Result 
 
TOE to have an adequate requirement for password policy. 
 
 
Obtained Test Result 
 
It is observed there is a strong password policy in the TOE require a combination of letters, numbers, 
and symbols with a minimum length of 8. 
Verdict: 
Pass 
 
8.2.7.1 Screenshots Proof

Report: Penetration Report for ULTRAFORMER MPT
Report No.: F690501-RF-CBS000062 
Version: 2.0 
 
 
CONFIDENTIAL 
 
 
Page 19 of 38 
 
 
Figure 9 Password error message 
As shown in Figure 9, TOE required a combination of letters, numbers and symbols. Evaluator further 
identified TOE required minimum length to proceed. The minimum length is deemed to be sufficient by 
evaluator with the combination. 
 
8.2.8 Application session timeout 
 
Application session timeout 
Test Number:  
PT-GPCB2509000039CS-008 
 
Description 
 
TOE implemented session timeout when TOE idle for a period. TOE allowed user to select the timeout 
in the TOE setting. To verify that session timeout has been implemented properly, evaluator have 
performed checks on session timeout. 
 
 
Expected Test Result 
 
The session timeout prompt necessary access control to access TOE interface. 
 
 
Obtained Test Result 
 
It is observed that the session timeout will prompt for necessary access control to access TOE 
interface.  
Verdict: 
Pass 
 
8.2.8.1 Screenshots Proof

Report: Penetration Report for ULTRAFORMER MPT
Report No.: F690501-RF-CBS000062 
Version: 2.0 
 
 
CONFIDENTIAL 
 
 
Page 20 of 38 
 
 
Figure 10 Application timeout in TOE setting 
 
Figure 11 Access control after session timeout 
As shown in Figure 10, there is a setting for application session timeout. Evaluator have tested multiple 
timeout settings and waited for timeout occurrences. As seen in Figure 11, the TOE have access control 
when a user wants to use the TOE after the timeout.

Report: Penetration Report for ULTRAFORMER MPT
Report No.: F690501-RF-CBS000062 
Version: 2.0 
 
 
CONFIDENTIAL 
 
 
Page 21 of 38 
 
8.2.9 Application account lockout 
 
Application account lockout 
Test Number:  
PT-GPCB2509000039CS-009 
 
Description 
 
To verify that there is lockout mechanism in placed to prevent brute force attacks, evaluator have 
attempted multiple invalid logins. 
 
 
Expected Test Result 
 
There is a lockout mechanism in place to prevent brute force attacks. 
 
 
Obtained Test Result 
 
It is observed there is a lockout mechanism in placed to prevent brute force attacks. TOE will lock up 
and required reboot if the number of invalid logins have reached. The number of invalid logins before 
locking out have been deemed to be adequate to protect against brute force attacks. 
Verdict: 
Pass 
 
8.2.9.1 Screenshots Proof 
 
 
Figure 12 Lockout mechanism 
As shown in Figure 12, error message shown after number of invalid logins attempts and was found that 
it is unable to enter any key other than reboot. Evaluator deemed it is adequate as lockout mechanism 
force user to reboot the system once invalid logins attempts have reached.

Report: Penetration Report for ULTRAFORMER MPT
Report No.: F690501-RF-CBS000062 
Version: 2.0 
 
 
CONFIDENTIAL 
 
 
Page 22 of 38 
 
8.2.10 TOE hidden network communication checks 
 
TOE hidden network communication checks 
Test Number:  
PT-GPCB2509000039CS-010 
 
Description 
 
To verify that TOE does not send information to developer backend servers unnecessarily, evaluator 
have performed network sniffing during TOE at rest and configuration changed when the license is still 
valid. 
 
 
Expected Test Result 
 
There is no sensitive information such as password or Wi-Fi information sent to developer. 
 
 
Obtained Test Result 
 
It is observed that there is no network communication with developer backend servers when TOE is 
connected to internet while license is still valid. 
Verdict: 
Pass 
 
8.2.10.1 Screenshots Proof 
 
 
Figure 13 Snippet of network communication 
As shown in Figure 13, there is no network communication between TOE and developer backend servers 
when TOE is at rest or configuration changed. Evaluator have performed multiple configurations changed 
as well as password changed while performing network sniffing. Evaluator analyzed the network 
communication and did not identify any communication between TOE and developer backend servers or 
with an unidentified server.

Report: Penetration Report for ULTRAFORMER MPT
Report No.: F690501-RF-CBS000062 
Version: 2.0 
 
 
CONFIDENTIAL 
 
 
Page 23 of 38 
 
8.2.11 Network connection via USB checks 
 
Network connection via USB checks 
Test Number:  
PT-GPCB2509000039CS-011 
 
Description 
 
TOE only provide Wi-Fi and does not have LAN port physically. To verify TOE having secure LAN 
configuration and does not allow LAN by other means, evaluator attempted to use USB to RJ45 adapter 
and connect to testing router for issuing of dynamic IP address.  
 
 
Expected Test Result 
 
If LAN configuration have been secure by setting static or disabled, TOE will not have IP address 
through LAN interface. 
 
 
Obtained Test Result 
 
It is observed that the TOE LAN configuration has been secure and unable to get IP address when 
connected to testing router. 
Verdict: 
Pass 
 
8.2.11.1 Screenshots Proof 
 
 
Figure 14 USB–RJ45 adapter connection 
As shown in Figure 14, the evaluator attempted to connect the TOE’s USB port to a test router using a 
USB–RJ45 adapter, but the TOE did not obtain an IP address from the router. Accordingly, the evaluator 
concluded that, since the TOE can only connect to the network via Wi‑Fi, this configuration is safe and 
appropriate from a security perspective.

8.2.12 Input via keyboard checks 
 
Input via keyboard checks 
Test Number:  
PT-GPCB2509000039CS-012 
 
Description 
 
To verify that TOE use keyboard to provide input or shortcut keys to bypass restriction in TOE.

TOE virtual keyboard. 
 
 
Expected Test Result 
 
There is no shortcut keys or bypassing screen input restriction that access into the system. 
 
 
Obtained Test Result 
 
It was confirmed that there are no known shortcut keys that can be used to bypass input restrictions 
on the TOE. Based on the evaluator’s checks, including various key combinations, it was determined 
that it is not possible to access the underlying system or escalate privileges using only the keyboard. 
Verdict: 
Pass 
 
8.2.12.1 Screenshots Proof 
 
Evaluator have plugged in a USB keyboard into TOE. Evaluator attempted using known shortcut keys but 
was only able to identify “Ctrl + Alt + Del” which will cause TOE to reboot. Evaluator was not able to 
escalate or access into underlying system when TOE reboot. Evaluator proceeds to test input restriction 
and wasn’t successful as TOE does not allow “Enter” key from keyboard. On top of that, virtual keyboard 
in TOE at multiple locations provided similar range of keys to enter. It is also seen that limited locations 
for user input which does not open possibility of command injections and the TOE does not use database 
to store information so there is no area to perform SQL injection.

8.3 Penetration Test Findings 
 
8.3.1 Excessive permission given to user account 
 
Excessive permission given to user account 
Test Number: 
PT-GPCB2509000039CS-021 
Description 
The evaluator confirmed that the user account has excessive access rights to multiple directories and 
locations that it is not originally supposed to access. This includes access to other user account folders 
and script files that reveal the TOE implementation, and under the current configuration it can not only 
view but also modify scripts belonging to other accounts. Such excessive access rights may allow an 
attacker to gain detailed knowledge of function implementations, increase the risk of exposing sensitive 
information such as backend service details contained in the scripts, and enable the attacker to tamper 
with the scripts and use them for subsequent attacks. 
 
Recommendation 
Limit the permissions that user has by implementing least privilege principle. 
CVSS Rating 
Vector 
AV:P/AC:H/PR:L/UI:R/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N 
Score 
3.9 
Severity 
Low 
CVEs 
None 
 
8.3.1.1 Screenshots Proof 
 
 
Figure 25 Files permission granted to user account 
As can be seen in Figure 25, the evaluator logged in directly with the user account and confirmed that there 
was a problem with the permission configuration. This account has been granted excessive privileges that 
allow it to view and modify other users’ home directories and files, and it contains many sensitive files such as

Report: Penetration Report for ULTRAFORMER MPT
Report No.: F690501-RF-CBS000062

CONFIDENTIAL

scripts, including start.sh and shutdown.sh. If these scripts are tampered with to redirect connections to an 
attacker-controlled server or to download malware and backdoors, this could result in serious security impact. 
However, the username has now been changed to a non-generic value, the SSH port has been disabled, and 
the password has been configured with sufficient length and complexity using a combination of digits, letters, 
and special characters. Since this password has also been checked against commonly used password lists 
included in “Seclists”, it is expected that, given these measures, an attacker would require a considerable 
amount of time to successfully gain shell access with this account. 
 
8.3.1.2 Delta Testing 
Excessive permission given to user account 
Test Number: 
PT-GPCB2509000039CS-021 
Precondition During delta testing 
Requested the developer to open the SSH port in the TOE, and the SSH credentials were shared by 
the developer. The SSH username is a custom username, which increases the difficulty. These 
preconditions were taken into consideration when calculating the CVSS. 
 
Obtained Test Result 
Test Date: 23 February 2026 
 
The evaluator logged in directly with the user account and confirmed that there was a problem with the 
permission configuration. This account has been granted excessive privileges that allow it to view and 
modify other users' home directories and files, and it contains many sensitive files such as scripts, 
including start.sh and shutdown.sh. If these scripts are tampered with to redirect connections to an 
attacker-controlled server or to download malware and backdoors, this could result in serious security 
impact. 
 
 
As it is low severity, it is an acceptable risk. However, the developer has been advised to fix the issue.

8.3.2 Configuration files exposed 
 
Configuration files exposed 
Test Number: 
PT-GPCB2509000039CS-022 
Description 
The evaluator identified that there are configuration files using user account and able to view the 
content. The configuration files can be quickly identified by its filename, wpa, which will relate to Wi-
Fi. The files disclosure Wi-Fi information which might leads to assets outside of TOE being 
compromised in TOE operating environment. 
Recommendation 
It is strongly recommended to restrict the access to configuration files to user and remove unnecessary 
files that is no longer needed.

As shown in Figure 26, evaluator identified configuration files that is related to Wi-Fi due to the filename. This 
will allow attacker to understand network and might possibly use the information to compromise assets outside 
of TOE in TOE operating environment. However, the username has been changed to a non‑generic value, 
the SSH port has been disabled, and the password has been configured with sufficient length and complexity 
using a combination of digits, letters, and special characters. The password has also been checked against 
commonly used password lists included in “Seclists”. Due to these measures, it is expected that an attacker 
would require a considerable amount of time to successfully obtain shell access with this account.

8.3.2.2 Delta Testing 
Configuration files exposed 
Test Number: 
PT-GPCB2509000039CS-022 
Precondition During delta testing 
Requested the developer to open the SSH port in the TOE, and the SSH credentials were shared by 
the developer. The SSH username is a custom username, which increases the difficulty. These 
preconditions were taken into consideration when calculating the CVSS. 
 
Obtained Test Result 
Test Date: 23 February 2026 
 
The evaluator identified that there are configuration files accessible using the user account, and the 
contents can be viewed. The configuration files can be quickly identified by the keyword "wpa" in their 
filenames, which indicates relation to Wi-Fi. This allows an attacker to understand the network and 
potentially use the disclosed Wi-Fi information to compromise assets outside the TOE within the TOE 
operating environment. 
 
However, the username has been changed to a non-generic value, the SSH port is disabled by 
default, and the password has been configured securely with sufficient length and complexity using a 
combination of digits, letters, and special characters. The password has also been verified against 
commonly used password lists included in "Seclists". Given these measures, an attacker would 
require a considerable amount of time to successfully gain shell access with this account. 
 
 
 
Hence, the evaluator concludes that although the initial identified issue has not been entirely resolved, 
the severity has been significantly reduced. 
As it is low severity, it is an acceptable risk. 
However, the developer has been advised to restrict user access to these configuration files and 
remove unnecessary files.

AV:P/AC:H/PR:L/UI:R/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:N

Severity 
Low 
CVEs 
None
